speaker: interviewer
text: Wednesday, March 12, 1997. You were talking about your experience in the fire hall.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, then things were getting hotter, like I said before. The-- the Ukrainian militia, pro-Nazi sympathizers-- there was good Ukrainians too, that were hiding-- but there was Nazi sympathizers. And they were looking for Jews. They found [INAUDIBLE] and her husband, and the daughter. They killed them. They found the rabbi hiding, and they killed him. They found another 10, 15 families they-- that they killed. And-- and they-- and the other ones that were looking for us. Somehow we had feelings, we could see from the straw hiding that they were walking. You could spot it somehow. So-- so anyways, we were laying-- another experience I will give you. We were laying in that barn, my brother and I. That's all what was left of the family. And we were laying in the-- pardon me-- in that hiding, in a straw pile on top of the ceiling. And we always went to the end where the farmer wouldn't take the straw, so he wouldn't catch us there. And that was also in the Czech's-- Czech village.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were laying there for a week. And after awhile, we hear there's movement in the next corner. And there was lots of times the Nazi sympathizers were hiding, if they-- and waiting for a Jew to come. So finally, there was too much movement in that corner. We finally got up. And there, it was our friend Nate Altimer, which he survived. So he didn't know of us being there, and we didn't know of him until the noise attracted us to him. So we spent a couple weeks together there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, again, we broke up, and we were hiding in different villages. Different-- we went to another-- [NON-ENGLISH], another Czech village. That was a-- a hilly village in the hills. And that one, we never stayed. And so we-- we knew that this village is getting hot, that they were getting on our tail, and that they were looking for us. So we went there and we stayed in about three different barns we were hiding. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I guess that's about the end. Then we heard the cannons, and the Soviet army was coming closer. And that was in '44, March, the Soviets came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in-- which town were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in [NON-ENGLISH] that time. A few days later, my brother and I, we walked. We came back into our town, Werba, and the Soviets were in town already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you return home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They-- well, our home was occupied by the Soviet Army. So when we came back to town, we got into the house and there was the officer's club in that house. So we met that Major, and he was Jewish. And he took an interest in us. We told him the story of what happened to us, that this used to be our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he made three rooms available for us in that house. He moved out the Soviet Army from three rooms. We had six rooms in the house. And these stayed so we got back to our house. And then-- you want me to continue with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, there was Nate Altimer came to town from the hiding. [? Laser, ?] the guy, him and his sister came back to town. And my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Erlichman, he was a schoolteacher, a Hebrew teacher. He wasn't my teacher, but he was a Hebrew teacher in town. He survived, Yankle Erlichman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then his brother came from the villages, that lived, came to town, plus another five from the villages that came, including from the villages, came a Jewish couple that survived. It was the pharmacist. He opened up a pharmacy in the town. So there was about a dozen Jews in that town and we were all sticking together. [PAUSES] And-- shall I go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was March, '44. The war wasn't-- wasn't too far, but 20 miles from our town. We could hear the cannons. And the Soviets, as soon they came in, they set up a non-military government. Their own people they brought in. Civil government, let's say, and the civil police. They set up their own. And under the Soviet Union, there was no private industry, so everything belonged to the-- to the government. Including in that government, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was the head of the trade industry. He was in 1939 to 1941 lived in our town when the Soviets came in. And he recognized us. So he helped us considerably. My brother got a job with the government. I went back to school. But in school, I was the only Jewish kid, and I was behind three years. And I was very, very depressed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I couldn't keep on school. I had to quit. It was no use, I couldn't learn. The Russian Major used to come every furlough from the front line. He used to come and visit us, bring us food, help us. And he was like a relative, really took a great interest into us. My brother, he was working. And he was caring for me. He was like the mother, the father, to me. He was doing-- he was the breadwinner. [PAUSES] Next, I-- there's other things that happened, which I cannot remember them exactly. But then, the major thing, next I went to a barber school for six months, and take barbering. Under the Soviet Union, it was easy to take a barber because every guy had from the bottom to the top, you just went with a-- with a-- with a razor, or the clippers and went from the top to the bottom. So you didn't have to be a stylist. So it was very easy, that barbering school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: 15. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we had a rough life. No-- only three kid survivors. Nobody to play with. Nobody to talk with.

speaker: interviewer
text: The other children in your town talk to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The--

speaker: interviewer
text: The non-Jewish children.

speaker: interviewee
text: The non-Jewish, they- they disowned us completely. They- and we were scared of them even after the war. There's a- there's a story complicated, which is not the-- the-- there was local bands that were against the Jews and against the Soviets. And they were invading the town at night, trying to kill the Soviets and the few Jews that were left.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Soviets gave us ammunition to defend ourselves if-- so we had a couple close calls that the local bands that were uprising against the Soviets were in waiting. We had to report right away and defend ourselves. So we had guns. And probably didn't know how to use them well. But we did have them. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's how we lived under fear from the local bands too, even under the Soviet regime.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then with the help of the Major, he told us that we would be better off to immigrate to Poland. Poland was also under the Soviet bloc. That was, already I'm talking end of the war, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you have any family, other than Jack?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was dead. And just five survivors in our town, plus 7-8 from the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The other town.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Surrounding areas.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So with the Major's help, [INAUDIBLE], he told us to immigrate to Poland. That time, the Soviets and Poland made a deal, anybody that was a Polish citizen-- because our country became Soviet Union-- anybody that was a Polish citizen up to 39 had a choice to leave. So they encouraged us to go to Poland. So all the Jews from that town, what 10 or 11, we all left in a-- by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was life supposed to be better in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll come to it. From Poland, it was easier to immigrate to the west. From Soviet Union, it was impossible. That's why we wanted to get out of that area, of this, also to leave the Soviet Union because our mission was to go to Palestine. So we immigrated to Poland. And in Poland, wasn't as strict as in the Soviet Union. Also, it was occupied by the Soviets, but when we came to Poland, the town was [SPEAKING POLISH]. And down there, there was already-- it was a year and a half after the war-- a year after the war-- they already had a- a Jewish kibbutz. They had quite a few kibbutzim from the Holocaust survivors. And we joined, my brother and I, kibbutz Dror in [NON-ENGLISH]. Life improved for us in the kibbutz. We felt safer. We had direction. We had somebody to care for us that was older. We had leaders. And we had kids our own age to play with from other towns, cities, survivors. We also went back to Hebrew school, not professional school, but learning anyways. So it improved considerably for [PAUSES] Jack and I. We had lots of incidents, the Polish bandits attacked the kibbutz. We had to defend ourselves. So it wasn't rosy completely, but it was a big improvement. From there, somehow, with the Jewish Congress connection with the kibbutz connection, we went to Germany. We went through lots of hardships to get there. We had to cross borders at night. We had to-- it wasn't easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you transported?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train, walking. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It took us a few weeks. And we go to Germany and we joined another kibbutz [INAUDIBLE]. Things got easier there in Germany. It was-- then it was occupied by the Americans. Things were pretty safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this kibbutz Dror was considered a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, kibbutz Dror was-- a kibbutz, getting ready for Palestine. So we joined that kibbutz Dror. And that was organized from leaders from Palestine that came and made speeches there. And so we were under guidance already. We didn't have to guide ourselves. We-- we-- somebody was taking care of it. And then we had to go for a- for a checkup. When we were freed, by the Soviets, never took us for a checkup, never. So then when they took us for checkups, they found I had spots on the lungs. I was-- had lung problems. And my brother had a heart condition. So that's what the war left us with, both sick. I was separated again from my brother. I was sent to one hospital, he was sent to another one. And I could have been in the hospital for about a year. And-- but we did see each other once in awhile. My brother, after he was released from hospital, he went to university, German minor university. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't think I took schooling. Then when I was released from the hospital after eight months, ten months, I went to-- there was-- we were under the guidance of Jewish organization, and United Nations, we were fed by. Then there came an opening, in '48, to go to Canada. Like we were called displaced persons then. Each country took so many in. And I was accepted to go to Canada. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Jack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jack went through school. He became an engineer in Germany. I made him papers to come to Canada. And by the time the papers came, he got married so the papers were invalid. In the meantime, he had an opening to go to the States. So he accepted that, and he went to Boston.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, there was an opening to come to Canada.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Canadian government took in 1,000 Jewish orphans. And I fell under that quota.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was [PAUSES] not quite 18. That was exactly [INAUDIBLE] was Jewish New Years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go when you arrived in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Halifax.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what you did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, we just stayed for two days. And there were so many openings each city. So the more religious kids, they stayed in the east. And we wound up in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And who looked after you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was taken in by a Jewish family here, Panners. And I stayed with them for four years. After awhile, I paid them board and room after six months. And I got a job right away. And I learned--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just general labor in the- in the wholesale clothing, and-- and then I got to improve and upgrading in the job as I learned the language. I went to college, night class, take English. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated when you arrived in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were treated very well because we were the responsibility of the Jewish Congress. Canadian government didn't vouch for us. We were-- the Canadian Jewish Congress had to get us jobs. And we couldn't go on welfare. We had to be dependent on the-- they were responsible for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people ask you about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never did talk about it too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even with the family you stayed with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much. I didn't feel good talking about it. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but as you get older, you want to leave a trace, maybe, to what happened. Maybe somebody will know. But it wasn't too pleasant to-- to talk and experience it. I very seldom talk to my brother about the happenings, and the experiences.

speaker: interviewer
text: After four years with the Panner family, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: After four years with the Panner family, I went and opened up my own store with another partner, Abe Goldstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of store?

speaker: interviewee
text: General store, clothing and groceries.

speaker: interviewer
text: And today, what type of work do you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Today, thanks goodness I'm retired. I'm 67.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any message that you would like to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's not much that-- I'm very-- I don't have any hatred towards the people. There were lots of good Ukrainian people that helped us. There were lots of other good people that helped us. But I regret, and I do not see how human beings [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] can treat children, babies, women, men, sick, old, shoot them, cold blooded, murder, and laugh. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It bothers me that the-- the killers that killed us in cold blood-- like it bothers me if I do something wrong, and-- and I can't see how come it don't bother them. After so many years, what they did in cold blood, murder, atrocities to human beings, that they don't come out and apologize or say we-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we did it, and we are sorry for it. Nobody came out and said sorry. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we human beings, do we deserve to be treated like this? [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I like to see that people, from seeing this, maybe they'll forget about hating people. Maybe that'll unite the people closer to know that other people are human. To have a little love towards other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mr. Manus.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Well, this picture is taken in 1946. On the left, it's me. And on the right is my brother, Jack, that survived. It was given to me last year on my birthday. My nephew enlarged it and give it to me on my birthday last year.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken the time when I was ready to come to Canada. To come to Canada, you needed medical clearance, and that was my medical report. The other one was a military passport of occupied Germany, which was American, British, and French. They were ruling Germany. So to come to Canada you had to have one of them passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please explain this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Starting from this side--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just the one [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a-- a big portion of our kids taking a joint picture together of [SPEAKING HEBREW] that was approved Israeli group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To go to Israel. Once you become a [SPEAKING HEBREW], that means you are already preparing to go to Israel, to Palestine. That was in 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Werba?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Werba.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1948 before I went to Canada. That was the three of us that survived our town. On the right, my brother, and myself, and then Nathan Altimer.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, before I was leaving for Canada. As a country, or Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe this picture then, please? The date and place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken place in Germany. And we are demonstrating against the-- for Israel to become a country. Israel wasn't a country yet. That's 1947. So I was in the parade there. This picture is made in 1946. And it's three survivors. The guy in the middle, [? Laser, ?] he was in the Soviet army later on. And he survived with us. And the left is his sister, and on the right is a lady that moved in from the surrounding district that survived. We were together in Werba after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the date of the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture's 1946-- in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and this one.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken [PAUSES] sometime in '47. And that was when we were in the kibbutz in [INAUDIBLE], early '47. That's all kibbutz [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This would have been in 1947 in Switzerland in the hospital. This picture is made outside of the hospital [INAUDIBLE]. But that was in the hospital [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Location?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember.
